topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, a jarring intrusion into a perfectly ordinary Tuesday afternoon. My breath hitched. I stared at the listing for 14 Willow Creek Lane. My childhood home. The words swam before my eyes—"For Sale." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like spilled ink. I clicked the link repeatedly, my fingers trembling slightly on the keyboard. The photos showed the familiar overgrown garden, the peeling paint on the porch railing, everything exactly as I remembered it. But the address, the very foundation of my past, was being offered up for purchase. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my living room, my heart hammering against my ribs with a frantic rhythm. Who would even be looking?

[story 2]

I had been scrolling aimlessly through real estate sites, trying to distract myself from a persistent sense of unease, when it popped up. My gaze snagged on a familiar street name, and then the listing hit me like a physical blow. My hand froze mid-air above the mouse. My knuckles turned white as I gripped the desk. This wasn't a prank; the details were too specific, too exact. A wave of icy apprehension washed over me, making the air feel thin in my lungs. I leaned back in my chair, my eyes darting toward the window, as if expecting someone to be watching from the street below. I needed to verify it, needed to know if this was some elaborate, malicious joke.

[story 3]

It was late, the house lights casting long, distorted shadows across the living room floor. I hadn't meant to look, but the online advertisement had caught my eye, and now it was impossible to look away. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant sensation rolling through me. I read the description again, the words seeming to vibrate with unspoken threat. My hands began to sweat, slicking my palms as I tried to process the implications. Every instinct screamed at me to close the browser, to pretend the screen was nothing more than a blank wall. But I couldn't stop clicking through the gallery of old photographs, each smiling face mocking the current reality of the listing.
